corpus.title: Ukraine drone attack sparks fire at Russian e-commerce giant's warehouse near Saint Petersburg
corpus.signal_text:
Ukraine drone attack sparks fire at Russian e-commerce giant's warehouse near Saint Petersburg

At least three people were wounded after a Ukrainian drone attack sparked a fire at a warehouse belonging to Russian e-commerce giant Wildberries near Saint Petersburg early Friday, regional officials said. Kyiv has struck five Wildberries warehouses since July 18, killing eight night-shift workers in a warehouse outside Moscow on Saturday and wounding dozens more.

Un entrepôt du géant du e-commerce Wildberries a été visé vendredi par des drones ukrainiens près de Saint-Pétersbourg. C'est la troisième attaque récente contre "l'Amazon de la Russie", accusé par Kiev de stocker et d'expédier des composants militaires sous sanctions destinés à la fabrication de matériel militaire.

corpus.title: Moment Ukrainian drones slam in to 'Russian Amazon' warehouse in St Petersburg in latest humiliation for Putin
corpus.signal_text:
Moment Ukrainian drones slam in to 'Russian Amazon' warehouse in St Petersburg in latest humiliation for Putin
- See more Daily Mail on Google - save us as a Preferred Source
Ukrainian drones Friday hit warehouses for Russian e-commerce giant Wildberries for a third time in six days, this time near Saint Petersburg, triggering a fire that sent smoke pouring over the historic city.
Kyiv has accused Wildberries - Russia's largest online retailer often known as the Russian equivalent of Amazon - of storing drone parts and helping the Russian army.
Ukraine has targeted Saint Petersburg as it steps up long-range drone strikes on Russian energy, military and logistics sites in recent weeks, calling the attacks justified retribution for Moscow's nightly drone and missile barrage of its cities.
Russia's defence ministry said its forces shot down some 571 drones overnight over more than a dozen regions and Crimea, the Black Sea peninsula that Russia annexed in 2014 - one of the largest one-night barrages of the war.
In Saint Petersburg, an AFP reporter saw grey smoke rising into the morning sky over high-rise residential buildings in the city, President Vladimir Putin's hometown that is more than 800 kilometres (500 miles) from the Ukrainian border.
Wildberries said two of its logistics facilities in Saint Petersburg and one in Simferopol, in Moscow-annexed Crimea, were hit.
'Rapid evacuation was carried out at all the warehouses ... according to preliminary data, there are no casualties,' Wildberries CEO Tatyana Kim said on social media.
Alexander Drozdenko, governor of the surrounding Leningrad region said three people were wounded in the attack, which saw at least 59 drones fired towards Saint Petersburg.

corpus.title: Ukraine hits ‘Russia’s Amazon’ warehouses outside St Petersburg
corpus.signal_text:
Ukraine hits ‘Russia’s Amazon’ warehouses outside St Petersburg
Saint Petersburg – Ukrainian drones Friday hit warehouses for Russian e-commerce giant Wildberries for a third time in six days, this time near Saint Petersburg, triggering a fire that sent smoke pouring over the historic city.
Kyiv has accused Wildberries — Russia’s largest online retailer often known as the Russian equivalent of Amazon — of storing drone parts and helping the Russian army.
Ukraine has targeted Saint Petersburg as it steps up long-range drone strikes on Russian energy, military and logistics sites in recent weeks, calling the attacks justified retribution for Moscow’s nightly drone and missile barrage of its cities.
Russia’s defence ministry said its forces shot down some 571 drones overnight over more than a dozen regions and Crimea, the Black Sea peninsula that Russia annexed in 2014 — one of the largest one-night barrages of the war.
In Saint Petersburg, an AFP reporter saw grey smoke rising into the morning sky over high-rise residential buildings in the city, President Vladimir Putin’s hometown that is more than 800 kilometres (500 miles) from the Ukrainian border.
Wildberries said two of its logistics facilities in Saint Petersburg and one in Simferopol, in Moscow-annexed Crimea, were hit.
“Rapid evacuation was carried out at all the warehouses … according to preliminary data, there are no casualties,” Wildberries CEO Tatyana Kim said on social media.
Alexander Drozdenko, governor of the surrounding Leningrad region said three people were wounded in the attack, which saw at least 59 drones fired towards Saint Petersburg.
It was the third overnight attack by Ukraine on Wildberries’ sites in the past week.
Last weekend, eight night-shift workers were killed in attacks on Wildberries sites that saw one of the company’s main warehouses outside Moscow burn to the ground.
Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky alleged after the first attack that the facilities were used to “supply sanctioned components for drone production and navigation equipment.”
Hundreds of small businesses saw their inventories wiped out in the strike, triggering angry comments on social media.
Kyiv calls its strikes “long-range sanctions”, saying the attacks are justified retribution for Russia’s more than four-year offensive and nightly attacks on its cities.
The campaign has triggered nationwide fuel shortages and, Kyiv says, dented Russia’s financial war chest.
– ‘Escalation’ –

corpus.title: Ukraine launches one of its biggest strikes on Russia this year, targeting Wildberries warehouses and a missile plant
corpus.signal_text:
Ukraine launches one of its biggest strikes on Russia this year, targeting Wildberries warehouses and a missile plant
Russia’s air defenses shot down 571 Ukrainian drones overnight on July 24 over 15 regions of Russia, occupied Crimea, and the Sea of Azov and Black Sea, the Defense Ministry reported.
Ukraine’s attack was one of the largest of the year, according to the Russian state news agency TASS. TASS put the year’s biggest strike at June 26, when the Defense Ministry reported destroying 660 drones overnight.
The total number of Ukrainian drones that targeted Russia on June 26 and July 24 remains unknown. The Defense Ministry only reports how many it shoots down.
For the fourth time this week, Ukraine’s attack included warehouses belonging to Wildberries, Russia’s largest online marketplace
Large fires broke out in the company’s logistics complex buildings in Shushary (St. Petersburg) and the Utkina Zavod industrial zone (Leningrad region) after the drone strike. The facilities have suspended operations, and a criminal case has been opened on charges of a terrorist attack. Firefighting helicopters are battling the blazes.
The strike on the Leningrad region warehouse injured several people, regional authorities said, putting the number at three. The Investigative Committee, Russia’s federal investigating authority, confirmed there were injuries but said the exact number was still being determined. Wildberries claimed that, according to preliminary information, no one was hurt at its warehouses in St. Petersburg or Leningrad region because employees had been evacuated in advance.
Two other warehouses in the Utkina Zavod industrial zone also caught fire, the independent Telegram channel Astra reported, citing photos and videos from eyewitnesses. One of the burning buildings houses a distribution center belonging to the “Petrovich” construction and trade company, while the owner of the second warehouse was not specified.
Local authorities said a warehouse at the “Severnaya” poultry farm collapsed in the village of Sinyavino, in the Leningrad region’s Kirovsky district, after a Ukrainian drone strike. The village lies near Lake Ladoga, about 40 kilometers (25 miles) from the Wildberries warehouse.
A Wildberries sorting center in occupied Simferopol was also hit, and eyewitness photos and videos show a fire broke out there. The company initially said the sorting center “was evacuated in accordance with safety requirements.” Wildberries head Tatyana Kim later

corpus.title: Ukraine strikes Wildberries warehouses in Russia for fourth time in a week
corpus.signal_text:
Ukraine strikes Wildberries warehouses in Russia for fourth time in a week
Ukrainian drones struck warehouses belonging to the marketplace Wildberries overnight on July 24, the company said, adding that it had suspended operations at its logistics complexes in Shushary, near St. Petersburg, and in the “Utkina Zavod” industrial zone in the Leningrad region.
St. Petersburg Governor Alexander Beglov said Ukrainian drones struck civilian infrastructure on Moskovskoye Highway, where cleanup efforts are now underway. He did not specify what kind of facilities were hit, though the Wildberries warehouse in Shushary sits on that same highway, and reported no casualties.
Leningrad Region Governor Alexander Drozdenko confirmed that a Ukrainian drone strike had set fire to Wildberries warehouses in the village of Novosaratovka, where the “Utkina Zavod” industrial zone is located, injuring three people.
A drone strike also caused a warehouse to collapse at the Severnaya poultry farm in the settlement of Sinyavino, in the Leningrad region’s Kirovsky District, the governor said. No one was hurt there.
Citing photos and videos from witnesses, the independent Telegram channel Astra reported that at least three buildings were burning in the “Utkina Zavod” industrial zone, one of them the Wildberries warehouse. Ownership of a neighboring building on fire is unclear, while a third houses a distribution center belonging to the construction supply company Petrovich.
Wildberries also said its sorting center in Simferopol, in occupied Crimea, “was evacuated in accordance with safety requirements.” overnight. The Ukrainian monitoring Telegram channel Exilenova+ claims Ukrainian drones attacked the warehouse, an assertion echoed by the local public page “ChP / Simferopol,” which reported the same. Exilenova+ published video of what it says was a fire at the marketplace’s warehouse there, though there has been no official confirmation of a blaze.
Other reports suggest a Ukrainian strike may have hit a Wildberries warehouse in Tver as well. Telegram channels published several photos and videos of a fire that reportedly broke out in the city following a Ukrainian drone attack, though neither local authorities nor Wildberries have reported any strike or evacuation there. Russia’s Defense Ministry, however, lists the Tver region among those where Ukrainian drones were shot down overnight.
This marks the fourth time in a week that Ukraine has struck Wildberries warehouses. Previous strik

corpus.title: Ukraine Strikes Wildberries Warehouses in St. Petersburg and Surrounding Region
corpus.signal_text:
Ukrainian drones struck warehouses belonging to online retailer Wildberries for the third time in less than a week, co-founder Tatyana Kim confirmed Friday morning, with the latest wave targeting a major logistics center in St. Petersburg.
Kim, Russia's wealthiest woman, said Wildberries facilities in St. Petersburg and the surrounding Leningrad region, as well as in annexed Crimea, were hit overnight. Fires broke out at several locations, though Kim said "parts" of the warehouses were saved.
While Kim said none of the company's employees were injured, Leningrad region Governor Alexander Drozdenko wrote in a post on Telegram earlier in the morning that at least three people were hurt during the attack in his region.
Images and videos shared on social media showed two huge plumes of smoke rising over St. Petersburg early Friday.
The Moscow Times was able to verify that one column of smoke was coming from the Wildberries logistics hub in the Shushary district, located roughly 15 kilometers (9 miles) south of the city center. The second was rising from the Utkina Zavod storage complex, located southeast of St. Petersburg.
In an earlier statement, Wildberries said that operations at two of its logistics sites in St. Petersburg and the Leningrad region had been halted. It also said that staff at its warehouse in Crimea had been evacuated.
Before Friday, five Wildberries warehouses, accounting for around 10% of the company's logistics capacity, had been attacked since last weekend, according to the newspaper Kommersant.
Ukraine did not immediately claim responsibility for Friday's strikes. However, following earlier attacks, Ukrainian officials accused the company of helping supply the Russian military with parts for drones and other equipment.
Together with smaller rivals, Wildberries and top competitor Ozon sell goods and services worth the equivalent of 8.5% of Russia's gross domestic product and employ 4 million people, or more than 5% of the country's workforce.
Reuters contributed reporting.
A Message from The Moscow Times:
Dear readers,
We are facing unprecedented challenges. Russia's Prosecutor General's Office has designated The Moscow Times as an "undesirable" organization, criminalizing our work and putting our staff at risk of prosecution. This follows our earlier unjust labeling as a "foreign agent."
These actions are direct attempts to silence independent journalism in Russia. The authorities claim our work "discredits the decisions of the Russian
